turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I just tried to return a Result from my handler and the compiler is screaming at me about IntoResponse. It feels like magic, why doesn't it just throw an exception or something?

It's not magic; it's how Rust's type system enforces a contract for axum. When you return a Result<T, E> from a handler, the compiler automatically applies IntoResponse to that error variant so the framework can convert your custom error into an HTTP response. You don't have to manually call it every time because the trait is baked into the request builder's signature.
Okay, but what exactly happens under the hood? I defined my Error type with a Display impl and everything else looks standard. Why did I have to implement IntoResponse specifically?

Because axum needs your error type to be convertible into an HTTP response immediately without extra boilerplate. When you implement IntoResponse for your custom Error, the framework can call it on any error variant returned by your handler and get back a Response object ready to send. It's just a trait that bridges your domain errors to the web layer.
Got it, so if I don't implement IntoResponse, my Error type is useless? Can't I just use standard error types from crates like thiserror?

You can mix and match. You might define your main error using thiserror for ergonomics when constructing variants, but you still need to derive or implement IntoResponse so axum knows how to turn that structure into a response. Often people use thiserror for the shape of the data and then add a separate IntoResponse impl just for the web layer.
That makes sense, I was confused about whether one trait covers everything or if there's a second step. And what about status codes? How does axum know which 404 to send?

The IntoResponse impl lets you map specific error variants to their own Status. For example, a NotFoundError can be configured to always produce a 404 response while a BadRequest might default to 400. You control this mapping in your trait implementation so the status code is part of the data structure itself.
So I can customize behavior per error variant? That seems powerful but also potentially dangerous if I get it wrong.

It is a double-edged sword. If you map the wrong status code, your client will see incorrect HTTP semantics and break their logic. But once you define the mapping correctly, every error variant carries its own response metadata including status, headers, and body content automatically.
Can I still use standard Result types from std? Does that break anything?

Yes you can. You define your custom error type containing the inner result or just use it directly as long as it implements IntoResponse. The trait is what matters, not whether the underlying value comes from std or a crate like thiserror.
I see now. So instead of fighting with manual error handling in my handlers, I just define types and implement IntoResponse once?

Exactly. You move away from manual try blocks and status code checks inside every handler by relying on the trait system. Your handlers return clean Result<T, MyError> values and the framework handles the conversion to responses for you.

I just started using axum for my backend and I thought I could just return any error type from a handler without worrying about how it gets displayed to the client. Isn't that what makes web frameworks easy?

That's actually a common misconception, but in axum you can't really do that because the framework needs to know exactly how to format your response. If you return an error type instead of using IntoResponse on it, the server won't be able to convert it into the correct HTTP status code or body content automatically.
So I have to manually wrap every single error? That feels tedious and goes against the idea of being lazy with boilerplate. Why can't axum just guess what I want?

The framework doesn't try to guess; it relies on you defining a custom error type that implements IntoResponse so it knows exactly how to render the response. You create a dedicated struct for your application errors and derive Debug, Clone, and Serialize from it.
Okay, but what if I just use Result<T, E> directly in my handler? Isn't that standard Rust and should work fine?

Result<T, E> works for your logic flow, but axum expects the final return value to be something that can be turned into an HTTP response. You need to map the Result's error variant through IntoResponse to get a proper HttpResponse.
So I'm still writing extra code even though Rust handles most of the plumbing? This isn't really making my life easier like I thought it would be?

You're feeling that frustration because you haven't seen how much boilerplate actually gets removed. Once your error type implements IntoResponse, axum handles the rest of the conversion automatically.
Can you show me an example where I map errors to specific status codes like 404 or 500 without writing custom logic?

You use axum's error handler to match on your custom error type and return different responses based on the variant. For instance, a NotFoundError could be matched against and send a 404 response while an InternalServerError would trigger a generic 500.
Wait, so I'm not just returning raw errors anymore? Is there some trait or mechanism that makes this automatic?
Yes, the IntoResponse trait is what bridges your custom error type to HTTP responses. You implement it once for your error struct and then axum uses it throughout.

I'm getting a panic in my production app right now and I need help. The error says 'cannot convert &MyError into Response' when I try to return it from the handler. My code looks like this: let result = get_data().await?; if let Err(e) = result { return Err(MyError::new(e.message)); } return Ok(response). What went wrong?

That panic happens because you're trying to return a custom error type directly without wrapping it in the right trait implementation. In Axum, handlers must return Result<T, E> where E implements IntoResponse or is an enum that does. Your MyError struct probably lacks that trait. You need to implement IntoResponse for MyError so Axum knows how to turn your error into an HTTP response with a status code and body.
Oh right, I thought any error type would just work since it's Rust. So if I add the trait implementation, will it automatically handle everything? Should I also change the return type of my handler?

You're right that Rust isn't magic and types don't just interoperate by default without explicit rules. But yes, once you implement IntoResponse for MyError, Axum can convert your error into a Response automatically. However, you shouldn't change the return type to Result<_, MyError> unless you want to catch errors yourself before returning them. The standard pattern is to let the handler return Ok(response) or Err(e) where e is already an IntoResponse type.
Got it, so I just need to implement IntoResponse for MyError? Should I also worry about mapping different error codes manually?

Implementing IntoResponse covers both cases. You can define a custom implementation that maps specific variants of your MyError enum to particular HTTP status codes like 404 or 500, while others default to 500. This way you control the exact behavior without needing extra logic in the handler itself.

Perfect, so my code should look something like this: impl IntoResponse for MyError { fn into_response(self) -> Response { match self { Error::NotFound => Ok(StatusCode::NOT_FOUND), ... } } } Is that right?

Exactly. That structure lets you map each error variant to a specific status code and response body. You can also use the Result<T, E> pattern in your handler where T is your data type and E is MyError, letting Axum handle the conversion automatically once the trait is implemented.
One more thing, I noticed my handler was returning Option<Result<...>> instead of just Result. Does that cause issues?

Returning an Option<Result> is definitely not standard in Axum and will likely cause unexpected behavior or panics. Handlers should return either Ok(data) or Err(error), never nested options. If you need to validate input before calling the handler, do that logic inside the handler itself using if let or match statements instead of wrapping everything in an Option.
I see, so I should just remove the outer Option and have my function return Result<Data, MyError> directly? And then Axum will handle the rest?

Yes, exactly. Your handler signature becomes fn get_data() -> Result<Data, MyError>, and once you implement IntoResponse for MyError, Axum converts any Err(e) into a proper HTTP response with the right status code.
Thanks! One last thing though, what if I want to return custom JSON error messages instead of just status codes?

You can include a body in your IntoResponse implementation by returning Response::builder().status(code).body(json(error_message)).into_response(). This lets you send detailed error details while still letting Axum handle the routing and conversion cleanly.
